seoTitle: How to Change a Power BI Data Source Without Breaking It
metaDescription: Learn how to change a data source in Power BI safely, so your visuals and reports stay intact instead of breaking when connections shift.
intro: Switching where a report pulls its data from is a common need, but it's easy to accidentally break every visual in the process. This guide explains how to change a data source in Power BI while keeping your existing reports intact. It's especially relevant for analysts who need to swap database connections or migrate to a new data pipeline without redoing all their work.

steps.heading: Review your current data sources
steps.body: Check the Data Source Settings in Power BI to see exactly which connections your report depends on before making any changes.

steps.heading: Follow the step-by-step change process
steps.body: Use the Transform Data or Data Source Settings menu to update the connection details, such as a new server name or file path, rather than deleting and recreating the query.

steps.heading: Watch for common challenges
steps.body: Be aware that column names, data types, or table structures that differ between the old and new source can cause errors even after the connection itself updates successfully.

steps.heading: Apply best practices for visuals
steps.body: Keep field names consistent between sources wherever possible, since visuals are tied to field names and will break if those change unexpectedly.

steps.heading: Confirm everything still works
steps.body: After switching, click through each report page to verify that visuals, filters, and relationships are still functioning as expected.

faq.question: Will changing a data source delete my existing visuals?
faq.answer: No, changing the source connection itself doesn't delete visuals, but a visual can show errors if the fields it relies on no longer exist in the new source.

faq.question: Can I switch from one database type to another entirely, like Excel to SQL Server?
faq.answer: Yes, but this kind of switch requires more care since you'll likely need to update the query steps in Power Query to match the new source's structure.

faq.question: What's the safest way to test a data source change?
faq.answer: Duplicating the report first and testing the change on the copy is a safe way to confirm everything works before applying it to your production report.

faq.question: Why do some visuals show blank after changing the source?
faq.answer: This usually happens when a field a visual depends on was renamed or removed in the new source, so it no longer has data to display.
